title: Sudden Blindness in Cats: Urgent Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat suddenly bumping into furniture? Sudden blindness is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Learn the causes and what to do next.
content_html:
What is Sudden Blindness in Cats?

Sudden blindness occurs when a cat loses their sight rapidly, often within hours or days. Unlike gradual vision loss, where a cat may learn to navigate by scent and memory, sudden onset blindness leaves them disoriented. You may notice your cat 'bumping' into furniture, acting startled when touched, or having widely dilated pupils that do not react to light.

Is This an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Sudden blindness is always considered a medical emergency. It is frequently a clinical sign of a serious underlying systemic condition. Common causes include feline hypertension (high blood pressure) leading to retinal detachment, inflammatory diseases, or neurological issues. Immediate veterinary care is essential to manage pain, prevent permanent vision loss, and treat potentially life-threatening internal causes.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, close-up photograph of your cat’s eyes can be incredibly helpful for a triage professional. A photo allows the veterinarian to see if the pupils are fixed and dilated, if there is visible bleeding within the eye (hyphema), or if the eyes appear cloudy. These visual markers help identify the severity and possible origin of the blindness before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Can my cat's vision be restored?
faqs.answer: Recovery depends on the underlying cause. For example, if the cause is high blood pressure and it is treated immediately, some cats may regain partial or full vision.

faqs.question: What should I do while waiting to see the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat in a small, safe area with their food and litter box nearby. Avoid moving furniture, as this will prevent them from further injuring themselves while disoriented.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of sudden blindness?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is high blood pressure, often linked to kidney disease or hyperthyroidism. Other causes include glaucoma, uveitis, or sudden acquired retinal degeneration.
